q: What is a repository (repo) in Git?
a: A repository is a project folder that Git is watching. On top of your normal files, it keeps a hidden history of every saved change, so you can see what changed, when, and go back if needed. You turn a plain folder into a repo with git init.
fs: A repo is a project folder plus a complete, versioned history of its changes that Git tracks for you.
fj: Thinking the repo is only the website on GitHub; the repo lives on your own machine too, and GitHub is just one copy.

code.label: start tracking a folder
lines: # folder is now a Git repo

q: What is a commit in Git?
a: A commit is one saved snapshot of your files at a moment in time, with a short message describing what you changed. You first stage the files you want with git add, then git commit records them. Each commit becomes a point you can return to later.
fs: A commit is an immutable snapshot of staged changes plus a message — a permanent checkpoint in the project's history.
fj: Treating commit like Save in a normal app; nothing is committed until you stage with add and then run commit.

q: What is a branch in Git?
a: A branch is a separate line of work that splits off from the main history, so you can build a feature without touching everyone's stable code. Your commits go onto the branch, and the main branch stays safe until you're ready to combine them.
fs: A branch is a movable pointer to a line of commits, letting work happen in parallel and isolated from main.
fj: Imagining a branch copies the whole project into a new folder; it's just a lightweight pointer, and you switch in the same folder.

code.label: make and switch to a branch

q: What does merge do in Git?
a: Merging takes the commits from one branch and brings them into another, usually pulling a finished feature back into main. Git combines both histories so the changes live together. If the two branches edited the same lines, Git pauses and asks you to fix the conflict.
fs: Merge integrates one branch's commits into another, joining their histories and flagging conflicts when the same lines differ.
fj: Expecting merge to always overwrite one side; it combines both, and overlapping edits become conflicts you resolve by hand.

q: What is a remote (push and pull) in Git?
a: A remote is a shared copy of your repo that lives on a server like GitHub, so your team can sync. You push to send your local commits up to the remote, and you pull to bring teammates' commits down to your machine. The usual remote is named origin.
fs: A remote is a hosted copy of the repo you sync with: push uploads your commits, pull downloads and merges others' commits.
fj: Thinking a commit automatically appears on GitHub; commits stay local until you push them to the remote.
